Popliteal sciatic nerve block + saphenous nerve block
For emergency category 1 (immediate threat to life)
TAP block (transversus abdominis plane) ± ilioinguinal/iliohypogastric nerve block
Plan A: Surgeon-placed epidural catheter (for major fusion)
Plan B: Erector spinae plane (ESP) block ± catheter
WALANT (Wide Awake Local Anaesthesia No Tourniquet)

Presentation Recommended Page Total knee replacement Total Knee Replacement ACL reconstruction ACL Reconstruction Knee arthroscopy (simple) ACL Reconstruction (local infiltration often sufficient) Patellar fracture / surgery Total Knee Replacement (adductor canal)

Presentation Recommended Page Ankle fracture ORIF Ankle ORIF Achilles tendon repair Ankle ORIF (popliteal sciatic block) Foot surgery / forefoot reconstruction Ankle ORIF (popliteal sciatic + saphenous, or ankle block)

Presentation Recommended Page Above knee amputation Above Knee Amputation Below knee amputation Above Knee Amputation (sciatic catheter ± saphenous) Digital / ray amputation Carpal Tunnel Release (WALANT / digital nerve block)

Surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque from the carotid bifurcation to reduce stroke risk. Performed via a longitudinal incision along the anterior border of sternocleidomastoid. Can be performed under regional anaesthesia (awake) or general anaesthesia.

Targets: C2–C4 superficial branches — greater auricular, lesser occipital, transverse cervical, supraclavicular nerves Approach: Ultrasound-guided, at the posterior border of sternocleidomastoid at C3–C4 level, deep to the investing fascia Local anaesthetic: 10–15 mL ropivacaine 0.375–0.5% Onset: 10–15 minutes

Allows continuous neurological monitoring (speech, motor function) during carotid cross-clamping — the gold standard for detecting cerebral ischaemia Reduces the need for shunting (only shunt if symptoms develop) May reduce perioperative stroke, MI, and death (though evidence is debated)

Combined superficial + deep cervical plexus block — deep block targets the cervical plexus roots between anterior and middle scalene muscles; more complete analgesia but higher complication risk (phrenic nerve palsy, vertebral artery injection, epidural/intrathecal spread) General anaesthesia with superficial cervical plexus block for postoperative analgesia Surgeon-delivered supplemental local infiltration — often needed along the carotid sheath even with a good block

Cervical plexus: C1–C4 ventral rami, emerging posterior to the sternocleidomastoid Superficial branches emerge at Erb's point (posterior border of SCM, junction of upper and middle thirds) Carotid sheath and its contents lie deep to the block — direct infiltration by the surgeon is often required for carotid manipulation pain Phrenic nerve (C3–C5) runs on the anterior surface of anterior scalene — at risk with deep cervical plexus block

Patient must be cooperative and able to lie still for 1–2 hours — careful patient selection Supplemental local infiltration by the surgeon is almost always needed, especially along the carotid sheath If conversion to GA is required (patient distress, surgical complication), the airway may be difficult due to surgical retraction and oedema Superficial block alone is as effective as combined superficial + deep in most studies, with fewer complications Bilateral procedures must not be performed simultaneously under regional (bilateral phrenic risk)
GALA trial. Lancet. 2008 Stoneham MD et al. Br J Anaesth. 2015
Partial or total thyroidectomy for thyroid nodules, goitre, or thyroid malignancy. Performed via a transverse cervical (Kocher) incision.

Targets: C2–C4 superficial branches of the cervical plexus Approach: Ultrasound-guided, at the posterior border of sternocleidomastoid, midpoint Local anaesthetic: 10 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side Onset: 10–15 minutes
Deep cervical plexus block — greater analgesic depth but higher risk (phrenic nerve, vertebral artery) Surgeon-delivered wound infiltration

Bilateral block required for midline incisions Low-risk technique with significant opioid-sparing benefit Useful as part of enhanced recovery — facilitates earlier discharge Monitor for signs of bilateral phrenic involvement (rare with superficial approach)
Shih ML et al. World J Surg. 2010
A searchable reference for common surgical procedures and their recommended regional anaesthetic techniques. #Blocks4ALL

Use the search bar at the top to find a procedure, block, or drug by name. Or browse by body region using the sidebar. Don't know the procedure name? Use the Find by Injury / Pain Site page to look up by presentation.

Essential pages you'll need on shift: LA Dosing & Calculations — max doses, concentration tables, adjuvants LA Toxicity & Lipid Rescue — recognition, Intralipid protocol, cardiac arrest management Standard Infusion Recipes — epidural, PVB, fascia iliaca, sciatic catheters Antibiotic Re-dosing — intraoperative re-dosing intervals by agent Contacts — pain team, RA on-call, useful links

Region Examples Upper Limb Shoulder arthroscopy, ORIF distal radius, carpal tunnel release Lower Limb TKR, hip arthroplasty, ankle ORIF, ACL reconstruction, AKA Trunk Laparotomy, mastectomy, rib fractures, caesarean section, spinal surgery Head & Neck Thyroidectomy, carotid endarterectomy

Plan A — the recommended regional technique with dosing Plan B / alternatives — for when the primary block isn't feasible Key anatomy — relevant landmarks and sonoanatomy Considerations — contraindications, practical tips, patient factors References — PROSPECT, NICE, primary evidence

Transfemoral amputation for peripheral vascular disease, diabetic complications, trauma, or malignancy. High postoperative pain burden — stump pain, phantom limb pain, and phantom sensation are common. Perioperative regional anaesthesia may reduce the incidence and severity of chronic phantom pain.

Sciatic catheter Targets: Sciatic nerve — tibial and common peroneal components (major innervation of the leg below the knee and posterior thigh) Approach: Ultrasound-guided, popliteal (if feasible given amputation level) or subgluteal/parasacral approach Loading dose: 20 mL ropivacaine 0.5% Infusion: Ropivacaine 0.2% at 5–8 mL/hr with 5 mL patient bolus, 30 min lockout Duration: 48–72 hours minimum — consider longer for phantom pain prophylaxis Femoral nerve / fascia iliaca block Covers the anterior thigh and medial stump. 20–30 mL ropivacaine 0.375%. Single shot usually sufficient; catheter if anterior pain is a significant issue.
documents.s: Plan A: Sciatic nerve catheter (popliteal or subgluteal) + single-shot femoral or fascia iliaca block

documents.t: Lumbar plexus block — covers femoral and obturator nerve territories more completely; higher risk profile Spinal / epidural anaesthesia — useful as primary anaesthetic in high-risk vascular patients; epidural catheter can provide ongoing postoperative analgesia Perineural catheter placed by surgeon — the surgeon can place a catheter directly on the transected sciatic nerve under direct vision

Sciatic nerve: L4–S3, exits pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen, descends between greater trochanter and ischial tuberosity For AKA, the nerve is transected at the level of amputation — the proximal stump is the source of stump and phantom pain Place the catheter as close to the transection level as feasible, or more proximally for broader coverage

Phantom limb pain affects 50–80% of amputees; perioperative sciatic catheter infusion for ≥ 48 hours may reduce incidence and severity Starting the block before surgery (pre-emptive analgesia) may be more effective than postoperative block alone These patients are often high-risk (vascular disease, diabetes, renal impairment) — regional anaesthesia reduces opioid burden and haemodynamic instability Anticoagulation: many vascular patients are anticoagulated — check safe block intervals Multimodal approach essential: gabapentinoids (pregabalin/gabapentin) started perioperatively for neuropathic pain prophylaxis
Bosanquet DC et al. Ann Vasc Surg. 2015 PROSPECT — no specific guideline for amputation; expert consensus applies

Arthroscopic anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction using hamstring autograft, patellar tendon autograft, or allograft. Tourniquet used. Graft harvest site contributes significantly to postoperative pain.
Targets: Saphenous nerve, nerve to vastus medialis Approach: Ultrasound-guided, mid-thigh, deep to sartorius Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% Duration: 12–16 hours
Preserves quadriceps strength — critical for early rehabilitation Similar analgesia for anterior knee pain Reduced fall risk

Femoral nerve block — excellent analgesia but causes significant quadriceps weakness; largely superseded by adductor canal block for ACL surgery Local infiltration analgesia (LIA) — surgeon-delivered intra-articular and periarticular injection; complements peripheral nerve block

Adductor canal lies deep to sartorius in the mid-thigh Contains: saphenous nerve, nerve to vastus medialis, femoral artery and vein The vastus medialis nerve branch provides sensation to the anterior knee

Hamstring graft harvest site pain (posteromedial thigh/knee) is not well covered by adductor canal block — consider multimodal adjuncts Early mobilisation and physiotherapy are priorities — motor-sparing block is key Single-shot block is usually sufficient; catheters are rarely needed Combine with paracetamol, NSAIDs, cryotherapy

Open reduction and internal fixation of ankle fractures — lateral malleolus (fibula), medial malleolus (tibia), posterior malleolus, or combination (bimalleolar/trimalleolar). Tourniquet typically applied to the thigh or calf.

Popliteal sciatic block Targets: Tibial and common peroneal nerves proximal to their bifurcation in the popliteal fossa Approach: Ultrasound-guided, lateral or posterior approach, at or above the sciatic nerve bifurcation Local anaesthetic: 20–25 mL ropivacaine 0.5% Onset: 15–30 minutes (sciatic nerve is large — allow adequate onset time) Saphenous nerve block (adductor canal) Targets: Saphenous nerve — sensory supply to the medial malleolus and medial ankle Approach: Ultrasound-guided, mid-thigh at the adductor canal Local anaesthetic: 10 mL ropivacaine 0.375%
documents.s: Popliteal sciatic nerve block + saphenous nerve block

documents.t: Ankle block — ring block of the 5 terminal nerves at the ankle (tibial, deep peroneal, superficial peroneal, sural, saphenous). Suitable for foot surgery; less reliable for ankle ORIF with proximal incisions. Spinal anaesthesia — appropriate as primary anaesthetic, particularly for emergency cases

Sciatic nerve bifurcates into tibial and common peroneal nerves in the popliteal fossa, typically 5–8 cm above the popliteal crease Block proximal to the bifurcation for reliable coverage of both branches Saphenous nerve is a pure sensory branch of the femoral nerve — supplies the medial malleolus via the infrapatellar branch

Sciatic nerve blocks have a slower onset than brachial plexus blocks — allow 30 minutes before assessing Popliteal block provides excellent postoperative analgesia (12–24 hours) Warn patients about foot drop during block duration — provide walking aids and advise fall prevention Thigh tourniquet tolerance requires sedation or GA; calf tourniquet is usually well tolerated with block alone Catheter insertion at the popliteal fossa is feasible for prolonged analgesia
Provenzano DA et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2005
Total hip replacement (THR) or hemiarthroplasty for osteoarthritis, fractured neck of femur, or avascular necrosis. Posterior, lateral, or anterior surgical approaches.

Targets: Femoral nerve, lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, ± obturator nerve Approach: Ultrasound-guided, suprainguinal approach — needle inserted lateral to the femoral artery, directed to lift the fascia iliaca off iliacus Local anaesthetic: 30–40 mL ropivacaine 0.375% Onset: 20–30 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

PENG block (pericapsular nerve group) — targets articular branches of the femoral, obturator, and accessory obturator nerves. Ultrasound-guided injection between psoas tendon and superior pubic ramus. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375%. Motor-sparing — may preserve quadriceps strength better than fascia iliaca block. Lumbar plexus block (psoas compartment block) — most complete coverage of hip innervation. Higher risk: epidural spread, retroperitoneal haematoma. Typically reserved for specialist centres. Spinal anaesthesia — commonly used as primary anaesthetic for hip arthroplasty. Intrathecal opioid (e.g. diamorphine 0.1–0.3 mg) provides excellent early postoperative analgesia.

Hip joint innervation: femoral nerve (anterior capsule), obturator nerve (inferomedial capsule), superior gluteal nerve, nerve to quadratus femoris (posterior capsule) Fascia iliaca compartment: between fascia iliaca and iliacus muscle Suprainguinal approach achieves more reliable lateral femoral cutaneous nerve block than infrainguinal

Fascia iliaca block is the most common perioperative nerve block for hip fractures (NICE guidelines) PENG block is motor-sparing but may not cover the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (lateral thigh incision pain) Posterior surgical approach: consider adding a lateral femoral cutaneous nerve component For neck of femur fractures, early fascia iliaca block reduces opioid requirements and delirium risk Anticoagulation must be considered — fascia iliaca is relatively compressible; lumbar plexus block is not
Steenberg J, Møller AM. Acta Anaesthesiol Scand. 2018 Girón-Arango L et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2018 (original PENG description) NICE guideline CG124: Hip fracture management
Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) for osteoarthritis, inflammatory arthritis, or post-traumatic arthritis. High postoperative pain scores — multimodal analgesia is essential.

Adductor canal block Targets: Saphenous nerve, nerve to vastus medialis, medial retinacular nerve Approach: Ultrasound-guided, mid-thigh, deep to sartorius Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.2–0.375% Advantage: Motor-sparing — preserves quadriceps strength for early mobilisation iPACK (Interspace between Popliteal Artery and posterior Capsule of the Knee) Targets: Posterior genicular nerves Approach: Ultrasound-guided, needle placed between popliteal artery and posterior femoral condyle Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.2%

Femoral nerve block — excellent analgesia but causes quadriceps weakness (fall risk) Continuous adductor canal catheter — for extended analgesia over 24–48 hours Periarticular local infiltration (LIA) — surgeon-delivered, complements regional techniques

Adductor canal: deep to sartorius, bounded by vastus medialis and adductor longus/magnus Saphenous nerve travels with femoral artery within the canal iPACK target zone: tissue plane posterior to distal femur, between popliteal artery and bone

Adductor canal block preferred over femoral nerve block — equivalent analgesia with preserved motor function iPACK addresses posterior knee pain not covered by adductor canal block alone Combine with paracetamol, NSAIDs, and gabapentinoids as part of enhanced recovery
Terkawi AS et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2017 Sankineani SR et al. J Arthroplasty. 2018
Intraoperative re-dosing of prophylactic antibiotics for prolonged surgical procedures. Re-dose at 2 × the half-life from the first dose, or sooner if significant blood loss (> 1500 mL).

Antibiotic Standard Prophylactic Dose Half-life Re-dose Interval Re-dose Amount Cefazolin 2 g IV (3 g if > 120 kg) ~2 hr 4 hours 1–2 g Cefuroxime 1.5 g IV ~1.5 hr 3–4 hours 750 mg Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV ~1 hr 2–3 hours 1.2 g Flucloxacillin 1–2 g IV ~1 hr 3–4 hours 1 g Gentamicin 1.5 mg/kg IV (single dose) ~2–3 hr Not usually re-dosed — Metronidazole 500 mg IV ~8 hr 8 hours (if needed) 500 mg Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV (infuse over 1 hr) ~6 hr 8–12 hours 15 mg/kg Teicoplanin 400 mg IV ~70 hr Not re-dosed intraop — Clindamycin 600 mg IV ~2.5 hr 6 hours 600 mg warning These are general guidelines. Always check your local trust antibiotic prophylaxis policy — specific procedures may have different agents and regimens.

Time-based: re-dose at the interval listed above from the time of the first dose (not from the start of surgery) Blood loss: re-dose after > 1500 mL estimated blood loss regardless of timing Renal impairment: extend intervals for renally-cleared drugs (cefazolin, gentamicin, vancomycin) — check local guidance

Set a timer or write the re-dose time on the whiteboard at induction If in doubt about timing, it's safer to re-dose than not (underdosing is the more common error) Antibiotic prophylaxis should be given within 60 minutes before skin incision (within 120 minutes for vancomycin/fluoroquinolones) Document re-dosing on the anaesthetic chart

Reported Allergy Likely Cross-Reactivity Alternative Mild (rash, non-specific) Low risk with cephalosporins (< 2%) Cephalosporin usually safe — discuss with patient Severe (anaphylaxis, angioedema) Avoid all beta-lactams Vancomycin or teicoplanin ± gentamicin Unknown / unverified Clarify before assuming Take a history — most "penicillin allergies" are not true allergies

Bratzler DW et al. ASHP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Antimicrobial Prophylaxis in Surgery. Am J Health-Syst Pharm. 2013 NICE guideline NG125: Surgical site infections Local trust antibiotic guidelines (check your intranet)

info Update this page with your local department contacts. Role Contact Pain team (daytime) Add number Regional anaesthesia on-call Add number Acute pain nurse specialist Add number Anaesthetic on-call (out of hours) Add number Theatre coordinator Add number Intralipid location Add location
RA-UK (Regional Anaesthesia UK) PROSPECT — Procedure-specific postoperative pain management AAGBI Safety Guidelines NICE Evidence Search

Local Anaesthetic Max Dose (mg/kg) Max Dose (mg/kg) with Adrenaline Absolute Max (70 kg) Onset Duration Lidocaine 3 7 200 / 500 mg Fast (5 min) 1–2 hr Bupivacaine 2 2 150 mg Moderate (15 min) 4–8 hr Levobupivacaine 2 2 150 mg Moderate (15 min) 4–8 hr Ropivacaine 3 3 200 mg Moderate (15 min) 6–12 hr Prilocaine 6 8 400 / 600 mg Fast (5 min) 1.5–3 hr warning These are maximum doses — not target doses. Use the minimum effective dose. Doses are additive when mixing agents.

Concentration Volume containing 1 mg/mL 20 mL contains 30 mL contains 0.2% 2 mg/mL 40 mg 60 mg 0.375% 3.75 mg/mL 75 mg 112.5 mg 0.5% 5 mg/mL 100 mg 150 mg 0.75% 7.5 mg/mL 150 mg 225 mg Example: 70 kg patient, max ropivacaine = 210 mg. That's 42 mL of 0.5% or 56 mL of 0.375%.
Concentration mg/mL 20 mL contains 30 mL contains 0.25% 2.5 mg/mL 50 mg 75 mg 0.375% 3.75 mg/mL 75 mg 112.5 mg 0.5% 5 mg/mL 100 mg 150 mg
Concentration mg/mL 10 mL contains 20 mL contains 0.5% 5 mg/mL 50 mg 100 mg 1% 10 mg/mL 100 mg 200 mg 2% 20 mg/mL 200 mg 400 mg

Calculate the dose in mg, not volume — a "safe" volume of a high concentration can still be toxic Doses are cumulative — if using multiple blocks or supplementing with local infiltration, add all LA together Patient factors that reduce safe dose: extremes of age, hepatic impairment, cardiac disease, low body weight, pregnancy (more sensitive to cardiotoxicity), hypoalbuminaemia Ropivacaine has a better safety profile than bupivacaine (less cardiotoxic, lower CNS toxicity threshold) Intravascular injection causes toxicity at much lower doses than subcutaneous — always aspirate and use incremental injection Ultrasound guidance reduces the risk of intravascular injection

Adjuvant Dose Effect Notes Adrenaline 1:200,000 (5 mcg/mL) Prolongs block, reduces absorption, marker for intravascular injection Avoid in digital blocks (debated), end-artery territories Dexamethasone (perineural) 4 mg Prolongs block by 6–8 hours Off-label; equivalent evidence for IV route Dexamethasone (IV) 4–8 mg Prolongs block by 4–6 hours Give at time of block Clonidine 1 mcg/kg (max 150 mcg) Modest prolongation Can cause bradycardia, sedation, hypotension
Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) can present within minutes of injection or up to 60 minutes later.
Perioral tingling / numbness Metallic taste Tinnitus / auditory disturbance Dizziness, lightheadedness Agitation, confusion Visual disturbance

Tremor → seizures → loss of consciousness Bradycardia, conduction block Hypotension Ventricular arrhythmias → cardiac arrest :::danger Important Cardiovascular collapse can occur without preceding CNS symptoms — especially with bupivacaine. Bupivacaine cardiotoxicity is particularly resistant to standard resuscitation. :::
Stop LA injection immediately Call for help
Manage airway — 100% oxygen If seizing: midazolam 1–2 mg IV or propofol in small doses Avoid large doses of propofol (cardio-depressant) Intubate if necessary

:::tip Intralipid Protocol (AAGBI guideline) BOLUS: 1.5 mL/kg IV over 1 minute INFUSION: 15 mL/kg/hr IV If cardiovascular instability persists after 5 minutes: Repeat bolus (up to 2 further boluses at 5-minute intervals) Double the infusion rate to 30 mL/kg/hr Maximum cumulative dose: 12 mL/kg For a 70 kg patient: Bolus = 105 mL Infusion = 1050 mL/hr :::

Start CPR — prolonged resuscitation may be required (LA cardiotoxicity can be reversed) Avoid vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and further local anaesthetic (e.g. lidocaine for arrhythmia) Adrenaline: use small doses (< 1 mcg/kg) — large boluses may worsen outcome Amiodarone for ventricular arrhythmias (not lidocaine) Consider cardiopulmonary bypass / ECMO if available and arrest is refractory

Location: Know where Intralipid is stored in your department — it should be immediately accessible in all areas where regional anaesthesia is performed Intralipid comes as 20% solution in 250 mL or 500 mL bags Can be given through a standard IV giving set — does not need a central line Start Intralipid early — do not wait for cardiac arrest Continue Intralipid even if ROSC is achieved — re-arrest is possible

Ultrasound guidance for all peripheral nerve blocks Aspirate before every injection and inject incrementally (3–5 mL aliquots) Use the minimum effective dose of LA Adrenaline as a marker: heart rate increase or T-wave changes suggest intravascular injection Know the maximum dose before drawing up Monitor the patient during and for 30 minutes after injection Have Intralipid immediately available wherever blocks are performed
All cases of LAST should be reported to: Local incident reporting system National reporting: AAGBI LAST registry
AAGBI Safety Guideline: Management of Severe Local Anaesthetic Toxicity (2010, updated 2023) Neal JM et al. ASRA Practice Advisory on LAST. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2020

Parameter Standard Solution Ropivacaine 0.2% + fentanyl 2 mcg/mL Loading dose 5–10 mL bolus, titrated to effect Infusion rate 5–10 mL/hr Patient bolus 3–5 mL, lockout 20–30 min Duration Typically 48–72 hours postoperatively

Assess block height (target: 4 dermatomes above and below incision) Monitor: sedation score, respiratory rate, motor block (Bromage scale), blood pressure If unilateral block: withdraw catheter 1 cm and rebolus If inadequate despite troubleshooting: consider conversion to IV PCA + rescue nerve block
Parameter Standard Solution Ropivacaine 0.2% Loading dose 15–20 mL bolus Infusion rate 5–10 mL/hr Patient bolus 5 mL, lockout 30 min Duration 48–72 hours
Thoracotomy, mastectomy, rib fractures, renal surgery Alternative to epidural when epidural is contraindicated
Parameter Standard Solution Ropivacaine 0.2% Loading dose 30–40 mL bolus (suprainguinal approach) Infusion rate 5–8 mL/hr Patient bolus 10 mL, lockout 60 min Duration 24–48 hours
Neck of femur fracture (preoperative and postoperative) Hip arthroplasty
Parameter Standard Solution Ropivacaine 0.2% Loading dose 15–20 mL bolus Infusion rate 5–8 mL/hr Patient bolus 5 mL, lockout 30 min Duration 48 hours
Total knee replacement Motor-sparing — preserves quadriceps for early mobilisation
Parameter Standard Solution Ropivacaine 0.2% Loading dose 20 mL bolus Infusion rate 5–8 mL/hr Patient bolus 5 mL, lockout 30 min Duration 48–72 hours
Above/below knee amputation (stump and phantom pain prevention) Major ankle/foot surgery Complex foot reconstruction
Parameter Standard Solution Ropivacaine 0.2% Infusion rate 2–5 mL/hr (subfascial) Duration 48–72 hours

Surgeon-placed catheter in the subfascial plane or wound bed Used for: laparotomy (rectus sheath catheters), caesarean section, breast surgery Simple to manage on the ward — does not require specialist nursing Evidence for efficacy is variable depending on catheter placement and wound type

Parameter Standard Drug Morphine 1 mg/mL Bolus dose 1 mg Lockout 5 minutes Background infusion Not recommended routinely (respiratory depression risk) 4-hour max 20–40 mg (adjust for age, renal function)

Daily review: assess analgesia, block height/spread, motor function, catheter site Catheter care: inspect insertion site daily for signs of infection, leakage, displacement Remove catheters: when oral analgesia is established and pain is controlled, or if signs of infection Anticoagulation: check local guidelines for safe catheter removal intervals with anticoagulants (applies to epidural and deep catheters) LA toxicity risk: remember cumulative dosing — infusion + bolus + any supplemental LA must stay within safe limits
Lower segment caesarean section (LSCS) via a Pfannenstiel incision. May be elective or emergency (categories 1–4). Regional anaesthesia is the preferred technique for both mother and baby.

Level: L3/4 or L4/5 interspace Local anaesthetic: Hyperbaric bupivacaine 0.5% 2.2–2.6 mL (11–13 mg) Opioid adjuvant: Diamorphine 0.3–0.4 mg or preservative-free morphine 0.1–0.15 mg intrathecally Target block height: T4 to cold/touch Onset: 5–10 minutes
Existing epidural: Rapid top-up with 15–20 mL lidocaine 2% with adrenaline 1:200,000 ± sodium bicarbonate No epidural in situ: Spinal anaesthesia if time permits; general anaesthesia if not
documents.s: For emergency category 1 (immediate threat to life)

documents.t: TAP block — useful when intrathecal opioid is omitted or inadequate. Bilateral subcostal or lateral approach. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side. Provides somatic wound analgesia for 12–18 hours. Quadratus lumborum (QL) block — posterior or lateral approach. May provide more extensive and longer-lasting analgesia than TAP. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.25–0.375% per side. Wound infiltration / catheter — surgeon-placed subfascial catheter with local anaesthetic infusion

Spinal anaesthesia at L3/4 is below the conus medullaris (ends at L1/2) Pfannenstiel incision: innervated by T11–L1 intercostal and iliohypogastric/ilioinguinal nerves TAP block target: between internal oblique and transversus abdominis

Intrathecal diamorphine/morphine provides excellent analgesia for 12–24 hours post-LSCS — this is the single most effective analgesic intervention Monitor for delayed respiratory depression with intrathecal opioids (especially morphine) Aortocaval compression: left lateral tilt must be maintained until delivery Hypotension is common after spinal — phenylephrine infusion is standard prophylaxis NICE and OAA/AAGBI guidelines recommend regional over general anaesthesia wherever possible
Kinsella SM et al. Anaesthesia. 2018 (OAA/AAGBI guidelines) Sultan P et al. Br J Anaesth. 2020 NICE guideline CG132: Caesarean birth
Open or laparoscopic repair of inguinal hernia. One of the most common general surgical procedures.

TAP block Targets: T10–L1 intercostal nerves within the transversus abdominis plane Approach: Ultrasound-guided, lateral or subcostal approach depending on incision Local anaesthetic: 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side Ilioinguinal / iliohypogastric nerve block Targets: Ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves medial to ASIS Approach: Ultrasound-guided, between internal oblique and transversus abdominis Local anaesthetic: 10 mL ropivacaine 0.375%
documents.s: TAP block (transversus abdominis plane) ± ilioinguinal/iliohypogastric nerve block

documents.t: Spinal anaesthesia — suitable as primary anaesthetic for open repair Surgeon-delivered local infiltration — complements regional block
For open repair, ilioinguinal/iliohypogastric block is often sufficient For laparoscopic repair, TAP block addresses port site and mesh pain Combine with simple oral analgesics for enhanced recovery
PROSPECT guidelines for inguinal hernia repair

Midline or transverse laparotomy for bowel resection, emergency laparotomy, hepatobiliary surgery, or gynaecological surgery. One of the most painful surgical procedures — effective regional analgesia is a cornerstone of enhanced recovery.

Level: T8–T10 for mid/lower abdominal surgery; T6–T8 for upper abdominal surgery Catheter: Threaded 3–5 cm into the epidural space Infusion: Ropivacaine 0.2% + fentanyl 2 mcg/mL at 5–10 mL/hr Duration: Typically maintained for 48–72 hours postoperatively

Gold standard for open abdominal surgery analgesia Reduces opioid consumption, ileus, and respiratory complications Facilitates early mobilisation and return of gut function Provides bilateral, continuous, titratable analgesia

Rectus sheath block — targets terminal branches of T9–T11 intercostal nerves. 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side, between rectus abdominis and posterior rectus sheath. Useful for midline incisions when epidural is contraindicated. Catheter insertion allows continuous infusion. TAP block (subcostal + lateral) — subcostal TAP for supraumbilical coverage; lateral TAP for infraumbilical. 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per injection site. Single-shot duration is limited (~12 hours); catheters improve this. Erector spinae plane (ESP) block — bilateral ESP at T7–T9. Emerging evidence as an epidural alternative. Motor-sparing; potentially less haemodynamic impact. Intrathecal morphine — 0.1–0.3 mg preservative-free morphine added to spinal anaesthetic. Excellent analgesia for 12–24 hours. Appropriate when epidural is not planned (e.g. laparoscopic-assisted cases).

Thoracic epidural space: narrower than lumbar; steep angulation of spinous processes requires a midline approach with significant cephalad needle tilt Rectus sheath: posterior sheath is absent below the arcuate line (below the umbilicus) — relevant for block technique TAP: between internal oblique and transversus abdominis; lateral approach at the mid-axillary line

Epidural contraindicated with coagulopathy, patient refusal, infection at insertion site, or haemodynamic instability Epidural failure rate is 10–30% — have a rescue plan (TAP/rectus sheath block, IV PCA) For emergency laparotomy, time and haemodynamic status may preclude epidural insertion TAP and rectus sheath blocks provide somatic analgesia only (no visceral coverage) — combine with systemic analgesics Enhanced recovery protocols (ERAS) strongly recommend regional anaesthesia for open abdominal surgery
Pöpping DM et al. Arch Surg. 2008 PROSPECT guidelines for laparotomy ERAS Society guidelines
Simple mastectomy, modified radical mastectomy, wide local excision ± axillary node clearance, breast reconstruction. Significant postoperative pain; chronic pain risk is high (up to 25–50%).

Targets: Ipsilateral thoracic spinal nerves (T2–T6) including dorsal and ventral rami Approach: Ultrasound-guided, parasagittal in-plane at the relevant thoracic levels Local anaesthetic: 4–5 mL ropivacaine 0.5% per level, or 20 mL as a single-injection technique at T3–T4 Onset: 15–20 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

Provides unilateral somatic and sympathetic blockade Equivalent analgesia to thoracic epidural with fewer side effects Evidence for reduction in chronic post-surgical pain Avoids hypotension, urinary retention, and bilateral motor block

Pectoral nerve blocks (PECS I & II) — PECS I: between pectoralis major and minor, targets medial and lateral pectoral nerves. PECS II: between pectoralis minor and serratus anterior at the level of the 4th rib, targets intercostal nerves T2–T6. 10 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per plane. Simpler and safer than PVB; good for less extensive surgery. Serratus anterior plane (SAP) block — superficial or deep to serratus anterior at the mid-axillary line, 5th rib. 20–30 mL ropivacaine 0.25–0.375%. Covers lateral chest wall; useful adjunct for axillary dissection. Erector spinae plane (ESP) block — emerging alternative to PVB with similar dermatomal spread, potentially safer. Evidence base still developing.

Paravertebral space: wedge-shaped, bounded by vertebral body, superior costotransverse ligament, and parietal pleura Breast innervation: anterior and lateral cutaneous branches of T2–T6 intercostal nerves, plus supraclavicular nerves (C3–C4) to upper breast Long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves — relevant for axillary dissection

PVB carries a small risk of pneumothorax (< 1% with ultrasound guidance) For bilateral mastectomy, bilateral PVB or thoracic epidural may be considered PECS/SAP blocks are fascial plane blocks — more volume-dependent and may have less consistent medial coverage Catheter-based PVB can be used for ongoing analgesia post-reconstruction Multimodal analgesia essential: paracetamol, NSAIDs, consider dexamethasone and ketamine
Schnabel A et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2010 Wahba SS, Kamal SM. J Anesth. 2014 PROSPECT guidelines for breast surgery

Not a surgical procedure per se, but rib fractures (traumatic or pathological) cause severe pain that impairs ventilation, coughing, and clearance of secretions. Inadequate analgesia leads to pneumonia, respiratory failure, and prolonged ICU stay. Regional anaesthesia is a key intervention.

Targets: Lateral cutaneous branches of the intercostal nerves (T2–T9) Approach: Ultrasound-guided, superficial to serratus anterior at the mid-axillary line, 4th–5th rib Local anaesthetic: 30 mL ropivacaine 0.25–0.375% Duration: 8–12 hours (single shot); continuous catheter infusion for ongoing analgesia Advantage: Safe, superficial, easily performed in the emergency department or ICU; low risk of pneumothorax

Erector spinae plane (ESP) block — posterior approach at the level of the affected ribs. 20–30 mL ropivacaine 0.375%. Covers both anterior and posterior rib pain; catheter insertion straightforward. Safe distance from pleura. Paravertebral block (PVB) — more targeted coverage of specific dermatomes. Can be placed as a catheter. Small pneumothorax risk. Thoracic epidural — best evidence for multiple bilateral rib fractures. Requires monitoring and nursing familiarity. Often impractical in polytrauma (coagulopathy, spinal injury). Intercostal nerve blocks — targeted but short-acting unless repeated. Multiple injections needed for multiple fractures. Higher pneumothorax risk per injection.

Serratus anterior overlies the lateral chest wall from ribs 1–9 SAP block targets the fascial plane between serratus anterior and the external intercostal muscles (or superficial to serratus) ESP block: local anaesthetic deposited deep to erector spinae muscle, superficial to transverse processes; spreads to paravertebral space via costotransverse foramina

Early aggressive analgesia reduces pneumonia, intubation, and mortality SAP and ESP blocks are safer than epidural and PVB in anticoagulated or coagulopathic trauma patients Catheter-based techniques preferred for ongoing pain management (rib fracture pain peaks at days 2–3) Often combined with systemic multimodal analgesia: paracetamol, NSAIDs, ketamine infusion, lidocaine infusion In elderly patients, rib fractures carry high morbidity — early regional anaesthesia referral is critical
NICE guideline NG38: Fractures — non-complex May L et al. Anaesthesia. 2016 Thiruvenkatarajan V et al. Anaesth Intensive Care. 2020

Posterior spinal surgery — laminectomy, discectomy, spinal decompression, posterior spinal fusion (instrumented or non-instrumented). Prone positioning. Significant postoperative pain, especially for multilevel fusion.

Placed by: Surgeon under direct vision at the end of the procedure, catheter tip in the epidural space at the operative level Infusion: Ropivacaine 0.2% at 4–8 mL/hr Duration: 48–72 hours Advantage: Placed under direct vision — avoids the risk of blind epidural insertion through operated tissue
documents.s: Plan A: Surgeon-placed epidural catheter (for major fusion)

documents.t: Targets: Dorsal rami of the thoracolumbar spinal nerves Approach: Bilateral, ultrasound-guided, at the relevant spinal level(s) Local anaesthetic: 20 mL ropivacaine 0.375% per side Catheter: Can be placed bilaterally for continuous infusion Advantage: Safe and superficial; can be performed preoperatively
documents.s: Plan B: Erector spinae plane (ESP) block ± catheter

documents.t: Wound infiltration / wound infusion catheter — surgeon infiltrates wound edges with LA, ± subfascial catheter for infusion. Simple and low-risk. Evidence suggests modest benefit for 1–2 level procedures. Intrathecal morphine — 0.1–0.3 mg preservative-free morphine added to a small intrathecal injection at the end of surgery (surgeon-placed). Excellent analgesia for 12–24 hours. Requires respiratory monitoring (HDU/enhanced care). IV ketamine and lidocaine infusions — useful systemic adjuncts to reduce opioid requirements. Not regional techniques but commonly part of the multimodal plan.

Posterior spinal surgery disrupts paraspinal muscles and periosteum — primary pain is somatic from the surgical wound and muscle dissection The epidural space is directly accessible to the surgeon during laminectomy ESP block targets the fascial plane deep to erector spinae — LA spreads to dorsal rami and potentially into the paravertebral and epidural space

Anaesthetist-placed epidural is generally not appropriate for spinal surgery — the surgical level is the target, and the anatomy may be disrupted For simple 1–2 level discectomy/decompression, multimodal analgesia without regional technique is often sufficient For major multilevel fusion, surgeon-placed epidural or bilateral ESP catheters significantly reduce opioid consumption Prone positioning limits some block approaches — ESP blocks can be done preoperatively in lateral or sitting position Discuss analgesic plan with the surgeon preoperatively — collaborative approach
Chin KJ et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2017 (ESP block) Dhaliwal P et al. Spine J. 2019
Open or endoscopic division of the transverse carpal ligament (flexor retinaculum) to decompress the median nerve. Usually a short day-case procedure.

Technique: Subcutaneous infiltration of local anaesthetic with adrenaline at the surgical site Local anaesthetic: 10–20 mL lidocaine 1% with 1:100,000 adrenaline Onset: Inject 30 minutes prior to incision (allows adrenaline vasoconstriction to provide a bloodless field) Duration: 2–3 hours
documents.s: WALANT (Wide Awake Local Anaesthesia No Tourniquet)

documents.t: No sedation, no tourniquet, no block-related motor weakness Patient can demonstrate active finger flexion intraoperatively to confirm complete decompression Very low complication rate Cost-effective — can be performed outside main theatre

Median nerve block at the wrist — 3–5 mL lidocaine 2% between palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis at the proximal wrist crease Axillary brachial plexus block — if bilateral or combined with other hand procedures IV regional anaesthesia (Bier's block) — effective but requires tourniquet and monitoring setup

Median nerve enters the hand deep to the flexor retinaculum between tendons of FDS and FCR Palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to the carpal tunnel — not protected by the retinaculum Recurrent motor branch to thenar muscles — variable anatomy, at risk during surgery

WALANT is now the standard of care in many centres for carpal tunnel release Adrenaline in fingers is safe — the old teaching that it causes digital ischaemia has been disproven No need for preoperative fasting with WALANT (no sedation) Bilateral procedures can be done in one sitting
Lalonde DH. Hand Clin. 2015 Nabhan A et al. J Hand Surg Eur. 2020
Open reduction and internal fixation of distal radius fractures — volar locking plate via a volar (Henry) approach. One of the most common orthopaedic procedures.

Targets: Trunks/divisions of the brachial plexus at the supraclavicular fossa Approach: Ultrasound-guided, in-plane lateral-to-medial, needle directed toward the "corner pocket" (junction of first rib and subclavian artery) Local anaesthetic: 20–25 mL ropivacaine 0.5% Onset: 10–15 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours

Axillary brachial plexus block — avoids risk of pneumothorax and phrenic nerve palsy; ideal when these are concerns Distal nerve blocks at the wrist — median, ulnar, and radial nerve blocks for targeted analgesia without proximal motor block; useful for postoperative analgesia alone (not suitable as primary surgical anaesthetic for ORIF)

Supraclavicular fossa: brachial plexus seen as a cluster of hypoechoic nodules ("bunch of grapes") lateral and posterior to the subclavian artery, above the first rib Pleura visible deep to the first rib — key safety landmark Dorsal scapular artery may traverse the plexus

Supraclavicular block provides reliable hand and wrist anaesthesia — "the spinal of the upper limb" Tourniquet tolerance is usually good for forearm tourniquets Avoid supraclavicular approach in patients with significant respiratory disease (phrenic nerve palsy risk ~1%) Can be performed as day-case regional anaesthesia without general anaesthesia
Tran DQ et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2009
Arthroscopic shoulder surgery — rotator cuff repair, subacromial decompression, labral repair, stabilisation procedures. Usually performed in the beach-chair or lateral decubitus position.

Targets: C5, C6 (± C7) nerve roots in the interscalene groove Approach: Ultrasound-guided, in-plane lateral-to-medial Local anaesthetic: 15–20 mL ropivacaine 0.375–0.5% Onset: 15–20 minutes Duration: 12–18 hours (with dexamethasone adjuvant)

Superior trunk block — reduced phrenic nerve palsy risk, similar analgesia for arthroscopic procedures Suprascapular nerve block — useful when interscalene is contraindicated (e.g. contralateral phrenic nerve palsy)
Interscalene groove between anterior and middle scalene muscles C5/C6 roots seen as hypoechoic round structures ("traffic light" sign) Phrenic nerve runs on the anterior surface of anterior scalene

Phrenic nerve palsy occurs in ~100% of traditional interscalene blocks — avoid in patients with significant respiratory compromise or contralateral phrenic palsy Consider low-volume technique (5–10 mL) to reduce phrenic involvement Catheter placement for open procedures or anticipated severe pain
PROSPECT guidelines for shoulder surgery Tran DQ et al. Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2019
